coredata.dc:title: Incorporating imine-based covalent organic frameworks nanosheet as an active filler for long-term nanofiltration desalination
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Wenqiao
Zhu, Jinyuan
Xue, Qiang
Zhang, Kaisong
coredata.dc:description: Covalent organic frameworks nanosheets (CONs) are two-dimensional porous nanomaterials exfoliated from covalent organic frameworks (COFs). The ordered permanent microchannels and excellent water stability of CONs make them attractive active fillers for the preparation of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) nanofiltration (NF) membranes. Herein, we report the fabrication of TFN membranes by incorporating covalent organic framework nanosheets TpPa-1 CONs as an active filler in the organic phase during the interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction. The addition of TpPa-1 CONs promotes the formation of extra water channels (pores and interfacial cavities) in the PA layer, rendering the TFN membranes with a higher pure water permeance of 8.54 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, which is about 1.4 times compared to TFC membrane, and excellent Na2SO4 rejection of about 98.1%. Owing to the hydrophilic nature of TpPa-1 CONs, the synthesized TFN membranes also exhibited higher hydrophilicity and superior anti-fouling performance. Meanwhile, the 35-days stability experiment verified that the TpPa-1 CONs TFN membrane has superior long-term stability. As a 2D porous nanosheets, TpPa-1 CONs provided some guidance for the preparation of long-term stability NF membranes with excellent separation performance.

head.ce:title: Incorporating imine-based covalent organic frameworks nanosheet as an active filler for long-term nanofiltration desalination

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Covalent organic frameworks nanosheets (CONs) are two-dimensional porous nanomaterials exfoliated from covalent organic frameworks (COFs). The ordered permanent microchannels and excellent water stability of CONs make them attractive active fillers for the preparation of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) nanofiltration (NF) membranes. Herein, we report the fabrication of TFN membranes by incorporating covalent organic framework nanosheets TpPa-1 CONs as an active filler in the organic phase during the interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction. The addition of TpPa-1 CONs promotes the formation of extra water channels (pores and interfacial cavities) in the PA layer, rendering the TFN membranes with a higher pure water permeance of 8.54 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, which is about 1.4 times compared to TFC membrane, and excellent Na2SO4 rejection of about 98.1%. Owing to the hydrophilic nature of TpPa-1 CONs, the synthesized TFN membranes also exhibited higher hydrophilicity and superior anti-fouling performance. Meanwhile, the 35-days stability experiment verified that the TpPa-1 CONs TFN membrane has superior long-term stability. As a 2D porous nanosheets, TpPa-1 CONs provided some guidance for the preparation of long-term stability NF membranes with excellent separation performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The problems such as water resources depletion and serious water pollution have prompted the search and development of more efficient water purification technologies [1]. Nanofiltration (NF), as an advanced pressure-driven membrane system, has been widely applied in water purification and wastewater reclamation [2–4]. However, it is still an essential bottleneck to enhance the water flux of the TFC membranes while sustaining a satisfying salts rejection [5–7].

Since 2007, Hoek et al. [8] found that introducing nanomaterials into the PA layer of TFC to fabricate thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane can enhance the water flux without sacrificing the salts rejection, and this has become a popular method for NF membrane modification. In particular, by introducing the nanomaterials as active fillers into the organic phase or aqueous phase during the IP process, the water flux can be boosted because the reactive filler will provide internal nanochannels [9–12] and external nanochannels [13–15] for water transport. In addition, the incorporation of nanomaterials can affect the hydrophilicity, roughness, zeta potential, and cross-linkage of the membranes, which will had significant effect on the permeability, selectivity, and anti-fouling performance [16]. Inspired by this concept, scholars have done a lot of related research to fabricate TFN membranes. You et al. [17] incorporated PEG-POSS nanoparticles into polyamide nanofiltration membrane to enhance the permeate flux and anti-fouling performance. Hu et al. [18] embedded graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets into the NF membrane separation layer via IP reaction to acquire a higher water flux with an unchanged high salt rejection. Sun et al. [19] synthesized TFN membrane by utilizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with different functional groups to achieve high permeate flux and anti-fouling performance while sustaining Na2SO4 rejection. Zhu et al. [20] obtained over twice the original water permeability by introducing modified hydrophilic MOFs into TFC membranes, while maintaining high Na2SO4 rejection. However, most active fillers tend to agglomerate during the IP process and are less compatible with polymeric PA layers, which will lead to non-selective defects of NF membranes, and the degradation of separation performance and service life [21,22]. Besides, the anti-fouling performance and stability of NF membranes are also critical bottlenecks that limit membrane properties [23]. The anti-fouling performance and stability of the membranes will directly affect the separation performance and lifetime [24,25]. Therefore, it is essential to find an active filler with uniform dispersion, excellent hydrophilicity, and good compatibility with the PA layers to fabricate TFN membranes with high water flux, superior anti-fouling performance, and long-term stability.

Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have been increasingly investigated in recent years in TFN membrane preparation due to the intrinsic nanopores (0.5–4.7 nm), ordered nanochannels [26,27], easy functional tailoring [28], and water stability [29]. In addition, the pure organic structure of COFs provided good compatibility with the PA layer, which can effectively reduce the generation of defects during the membrane fabrication [30,31]. Nevertheless, the size of COFs particles (1–3 μm) may not match with the thickness of the PA layer (7–300 nm) [32]. Hence there still exist a size limitation in the application of COFs-modified NF membranes. The exfoliation of covalent organic frameworks into covalent organic nanosheets (CONs) not only effectively reduces the size of the nanosheets, but also helps to expose the active sites. Suman et al. [33] found that mechanically exfoliated COFs could maintain the structural integrity, and the obtained 2D nanosheets (2D-CONs) are stable in aqueous, acidic, and alkaline media. Since then, researchers have been mechanically exfoliating COFs particles to obtain nanosheets for NF membranes preparation. Jiang et al. [34] added covalent triazine skeletal nanosheets (CTN) to the aqueous phase solution to participate in the IP reaction between PIP and TMC, and obtained NF membranes with a 5-fold increase in flux compared to the TFC membranes, while maintaining the Na2SO4 rejection higher than 95%. Li et al. [35]selected hydrophilic triazine structured COFs nanosheets (NENP-1) to obtain nanocomposite membranes with high water flux and salt rejection by exploiting the synergistic effect of NENP-1 with good pore size, hydrophilicity and positive charge capability.

As imine-based COFs, TpPa-1 were abundant with oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups, thus exhibiting high hydrophilicity and electronegativity [33]. In addition, it was found that TpPa-1 COFs have higher crystallinity and chemical stability than other COFs [36], and can form covalent bonds with PA layers to enhance compatibility with polymers, making them ideally suited for fabricating TFN membranes with outstanding performance. Furthermore, the TpPa-1 CONs obtained by mechanical grinding exfoliation showed better compatibility with PA layer while maintaining the porosity, crystallinity and stability.

Herein, we synthesized imine-based TpPa-1 CONs and integrated it into the PA layer by IP reaction. Thus, the nano-pore size, 2D structure, hydrophilicity and electronegativity of TpPa-1 CONs can be fully utilized to improve the water permeance and Na2SO4 rejection. The TpPa-1 CONs TFN membranes demonstrated excellent long-term (35-days) stability. This experiment broadens the scope of the application of TpPa-1 COFs and provides a new way to prepare nanocomposite NF membranes.

PSf (Polysulfone) pellets were bought from Solvay Advanced Polymers, Shanghai, China. Monomers Tp (1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol, ≥97%) and Pa-1 (p-phenylenediamine, ≥99%) for synthesis of TpPa-1 COFs were bought from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China. The PIP (piperazine, ≥99%), TEA (triethylamine, ≥99%), CSA ((±)-Camphor-10-sulfonic acid, ≥99%), SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfonate, ≥99%), TMC (1,3,5-benzenetrimethyl acid chloride, ≥98%) and n-hexane (≥97%) were provided by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China to synthesis TFC membrane. Na2SO4 (sodium sulfate, ≥99%), MgSO4 (magnesium sulfate, ≥98%), NaCl (sodium chloride, ≥99.5%), MgCl2 (magnesium chloride, ≥99.5%) and PEG (Polyethylene glycol) with different molecular weights of 200, 300, 400, 600, 1000 Da were used for testing the salt rejection and Molecular Weight Cut Off (MWCO), all above were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China. Deionized water (DI, conductivity <10 μs/cm) was used throughout the experiments.

The TpPa-1 COFs were synthesized by a conventional solvothermal method with a aldehyde amine condensation reaction according to the method of Kandambeth et al. [37] with few modifications. Specifically, Tp (0.12 mmol, 25.2 mg) and Pa-1 (0.18 mmol, 19.2 mg) were dispersed in 8 ml ethanol, respectively. Then the homogeneous solutions obtained by ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min were transferred to a Teflon autoclave. After replenished with nitrogen, the Teflon autoclave was sealed off, and heated at 120 °C for 72 h. After recovering to room temperature, the powder obtained by filtration was then solvent exchanged with N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and ethanol in turn, and dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 12 h to obtain a deep red powder named TpPa-1 COFs, the schematic diagram was given in Fig. S1A.

TpPa-1 CONs were exfoliated from TpPa-1 COFs by mechanical grinding methods [33]. Specifically, 50 mg of TpPa-1 COFs were placed in a mortar (i.d. = 75 mm) and ground continuously for 30 min after adding 1–2 drops of methanol. The powder was collected and dispersed in 100 ml of methanol, centrifuged at 7000 rpm/min for 10 min, and dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 12 h to obtain the TpPa-1 CONs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of TFC and TpPa-1 CONs nanocomposite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Our laboratory has reported the preparation of the PSf substrate used in this work (Fig. S2) [38]. Before the experiment, the residual NaHSO3, which was used to maintain the freshness of the PSf substrates, was removed through immersing the PSf substrates in DI water over 12 h. Thereafter, the PSf substrate was pasted on the epoxy frame by double-sided paper tape (Fig. S3) gently and the water droplet was mopped off with soft paper towel meticulously [39]. For the IP reaction, the aqueous phase solution was made by dissolving PIP, CSA, TEA and SDS in DI water with the concentration of 1.00 wt%, 1.50 wt%, 1.50 wt% and 0.05 wt%, the organic phase solution was made by dissolving 0.15 wt/v% TMC in n-hexane [40]. Different mass fractions (0.004, 0.006, 0.007, 0.009 and 0.010 wt/v%) of TpPa-1 CONs were sonicated and dispersed into the above organic phase solution for the preparation of TFN membranes. The TFC membranes without TpPa-1 CONs were noted as M1, and the TFN membranes containing TpPa-1 CONs were noted as M2-M6 in sequence. Scheme 1 illustrated the main synthesis process of M1-M6. Firstly, 50 ml aqueous solution was poured on the PSf substrate surface and kept for 80 s, then the aqueous phase that was not adsorbed by the substrate was poured off and was dried at room temperature. Subsequently, 50 ml organic phase (containing varying concentrations of TpPa-1 CONs) was slowly deposited on the dried substrate surface for 5 s, and then the membrane was heated in the oven at 50 °C for 1 min to intensify the IP reaction. Lastly, the prepared membranes were rinsed and stored in DI water (4 °C) for characterization and performance test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TpPa-1 COFs and TpPa-1 CONs
ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic morphology and structure of TpPa-1 CONs were characterized by FE-SEM (Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscope, S-4800, HITACHI, Japan) under 5 kV operating voltage and 10 μA operating current, EDX (Oxford 6587, HITACHI, Japan), TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy, Talos-S, FEI, USA) under 100 kV operating voltage and 20 μA operating current. It is notable that the samples for TEM test were prepared as follows: first sonicated 1 mg TpPa-1 CONs in 10 mL isopropanol for 5 min, then coated the solution on the carbon-coated copper grid (TEM), and dried them at room temperature for test. The particle size distribution and zeta potential of TpPa-1 CONs was characterized by ZetaPALS (Zetasizer Nano S90, Malvern Panalytical, UK). And the thicknesses of the nanoparticles were tested by AFM (Atomic Force Microscope, Dimension Icon, Bruker, USA). The molecular structure of TpPa-1 CONs was characterized by FT-IR Spectrometer (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer, iS10, Thermo, USA). The crystal structure of TpPa-1 CONs was tested by P-XRD (Powder X-ray diffraction, X'Pert Pro, PANalytical, The Netherlands) under 40 kV operating voltage and 40 mA operating current. The atomic composition and content of TpPa-1 COFs and TpPa-1 CONs were tested by XPS (X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy, Axis Supra, Kratos, Britain). The interfacial polymerization reactions between TpPa-1 CONs and TMC were characterized by FT-IR and XPS. The microscopic morphology and structure of TpPa-1 COFs were given in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TFC and TpPa-1 CONs nanocomposite membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
The microstructure of the membranes, including the top-surface and cross-sectional morphology, were characterized by FE-SEM (under 5 kV, 10 μA) and TEM (100 kV, 20 μA). The distribution of TpPa-1 CONs was analyzed by cross-sectional fluorescence images obtained with LSCM (Laser Scanning spectral Confocal Microscope, Leica TCS SP2, Leica Microsystems). The surface roughness of M1-M6 were examined by AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy, Dimension 3100, Bruker, USA). The water contact angle, which represents the hydrophilicity of the membranes with DI water were evaluated by DSA100 (Contact Angle Analyzer, KRUSS, German). The surface charge (from pH = 3 to 11, adjusted by 0.05 M HCl and NaOH) of the membranes was tested by SurPASS 3 (Zeta potential, Anton Paar, Australia). In order to obtain accurate test results, it was necessary to use 0.01 M KCl as the background electrolyte solution, with the gap height maintained at about 100 μm. The atomic composition of the membranes surface was also tested by XPS, where the degree of cross-linking value of the PA layer can be calculated using the following Eqs. (1)–(3):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of TpPa-1 CONs NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
As reported in previous studies by our group [38], the separation performance of membrane mainly include the pure water permeance and the salt (Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, and NaCl) rejection. The feed solution for the former is DI water and for the latter is a 2000 ppm salt solution, respectively. The experimental procedure was performed using a laboratory self-assembled dead-end filtration apparatus that consisted mainly of a nitrogen bottle (to provide operate pressure), a buffer bottle (to contain the feed solution), an ultrafiltration cup (to carry out the nanofiltration process, AIMCO Stirred Cell, Millipore) as well as an electronic digital weighing balance (to measure the weight of the permeance). The schematic process of the dead-end filtration system setup is shown in Fig. S4. Prior to tests, each membrane was normalized by pre-compressed for 30 min under the pressure of 4.0 bar to ensure a steady state of the system. The pure water permeance was measured by recording the change of weight data obtained from the analytical balance by computer, and then calculated by Eq. (4):

The permeance was collected with a computer program software while the conductivity of the feed solution and the permeate solution were both tested by conductivity meter (DDS307A, INESA). ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and anti-fouling performance of TpPa-1 CONs NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of M1 and M4 were tested for 35-days using 2000 ppm Na2SO4 as feed solution. The permeance was collected more than 6 times per day, the permeance and Na2SO4 rejection were calculated by Eq. (4) and Eq. (6).

The anti-fouling test, DI water was used as the feed solution firstly for the filtration experiment of M1 and M4 at 4.0 bar for 60 min, and the stable pure water permeance was defined as the initial permeance (
J 0
). Then the feed solution was changed into 1000 ppm BSA for 60 min NF experiment. The polluted membrane was washed with DI water to remove the remaining contaminants on the surface. Finally, the membrane was filtered with DI water for 30 min to achieve a stable water flux (J). Steps 2–3 was repeated for three cycles. The anti-fouling performance of the membrane was represented by the flux recovery rate (FRR), which can be calculated by Eq. (8).
(8)

( % )
=
J

× 100

Before the membrane fabrication, the 60 min ultrasonic dispersion-static experiment were utilized to explore the TpPa-1 CONs dispersion in the aqueous phase and the organic phase. As shown in Fig. S5, the dispersion of TpPa-1 CONs in the organic phase was more quickly and stable. In contrast, in the aqueous phase, the solid particles were still visible after 60 min of sonication and aggregated severely after leaving in a static position. Therefore, TpPa-1 CONs were dispersed in the organic phase in the subsequent membrane preparation process.

The physiochemical properties of TpPa-1 CONs were characterized by FE-SEM, EDX, TEM, AFM, FT-IR, XRD and XPS. The reaction between TpPa-1 CONs and TMC was characterized by FT-IR and XPS. The results were presented in Fig. 1 . The FE-SEM and EDX images in Fig. 1A showed that the morphology of TpPa-1 CONs was laminar and mainly composed of C, N, and O, which was consistent with the results of TpPa-1 COFs (Fig. S6A). The TEM results also demonstrated the lamellar morphology (Fig. 1B). The upper right corner of Fig. 1B showed that the particle lateral size distributions of the TpPa-1 CONs in n-hexane measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) method with a laser particle size analyzer was mainly distributed in the range of 400–800 nm, and the average lateral size was about 583.3 nm, which was smaller than that of TpPa-1 COFs (700–1300 nm, average lateral size ∼1161.0 nm, Fig. S6B upper right corner) and consistent with TEM image results. The bottom right corner of Fig. 1B showed that the zeta potential of the TpPa-1 CONs was −27.6 mV at pH = 7, which was more negative than that of TpPa-1 COFs (−16.4 mV at pH = 7, Fig. S6B bottom right corner). This is due to the fact that mechanical grinding exposes more active sites of COFs and therefore is more electronegative. Incidentally, this would enhance the electronegativity of the NF membrane and improve the Na2SO4 rejection. The AFM results (Fig. 1C) revealed that mechanical grinding significantly reduced the thickness of the TpPa-1 CONs from about 7.63 nm (Fig. S6C) to 2.66 nm. Thin thicknesses and large lateral sizes make TpPa-1 CONs easier to lay flat on the PA layer [42]. The chemical structure characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 1D) demonstrated that the characteristic peaks of the TpPa-1 CONs are not significantly different from the TpPa-1 COFs. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S6D, the new stretching vibrational bands, and the disappearance stretching band of Tp and stretching vibration band of Pa-1 indicated that the monomer is completely consumed [43]. All observed characteristic peaks correspond with reported results which means the synthesis was successful [35]. The XRD pattern showed the main diffraction peaks of TpPa-1 CONs at 4.79°, 8.54°, and 27.04° in Fig. 1E, which correspond to the 100, 110, and 001 plane, respectively. These diffraction peaks also remain essentially unchanged after exfoliation. The full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 1F) revealed that TpPa-1 CONs was consisted of C, N and O elements, which was consistent with the results of EDX, and their contents were calculated. Detailed spectral analysis of XPS were given in Fig. S7. These characterizations suggested that exfoliating the TpPa-1 COFs by mechanical grinding allows them to maintain their original crystal structure, chemical structure, while becoming more compatible with the PA layer, thus facilitating the reduction of non-selective defects during the IP process.

The polymerization reaction between TpPa-1 CONs and TMC were verified by FT-IR and XPS. A certain amount of TpPa-1 CONs was added into the n-hexane solution of TMC (500 ppm). After stirring at 400 rpm/min for 30 min, the product was washed by n-hexane for three times and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for characterization. The FT-IR showed the formation of amide bonds (N–C–O) at 1627 cm−1 (Fig. 2 A) [44]. The XPS spectra of N1s in Fig. 2B for the reaction products could be divided into peaks at the binding energy of 397.83 and 401.24 eV, corresponding to the N–H and N–C–O, respectively [45]. These results indicated that TpPa-1 CONs reacts with TMC to form covalent bonds during IP process (Fig. S1B), and resulting in better compatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TpPa-1 CONs nanocomposite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the M1-M6 membranes were observed under SEM at 30,000 × and 20,000 × , respectively. The reaction of PIP and TMC formed a relatively smooth PA layer, which displayed only a few spherical nodules evenly distributed on the surface (Fig. 3 A1-B1) [46]. A random distribution of TpPa-1 CONs can be observed, which are marked with red circles in Fig. 3 A2-B6. By the result of SEM, it can be observed that TpPa-1 CONs were successfully incorporated into the PA layer. This is resemble to the morphology of the TpPa-1 CONs TFN membrane observed in previous literatures [37]. The surface morphology of the M1-M6 membranes under SEM at 10,000 × and 25,000 × can be seen from Fig. S8.

The successful incorporation of TpPa-1 CONs was observed by TEM and LSCM. Fig. 4 A shown that the PA layer was attached on the PSf substrate. Fig. 4B–C demonstrated the successful incorporation of the TpPa-1 CONs into the PA layer. At the same time, the incorporation of the nanosheets resulted in an increased number of nanopores on the membrane surface, and decreases the thickness of PA layer (from ∼175 to 194 nm to ∼92–102 nm), which in turn had a strong water transport capacity. The presence of TpPa-1 CONs was characterized by LSCM taking advantage of the fluorescence of TpPa-1 [36]. As shown in Fig. 4D, the pristine TFC membrane did not show any fluorescence. After the incorporation of TpPa-1 CONs, green fluorescence appeared throughout the cross sections (Fig. 4E). This result provides direct evidence for the incorporation of TpPa-1 into PA layer. However, it should be noted that the brightness as well as the contrast presented by the images are only limited by the flatness of the cross-section, instead demonstrating the distribution of the TpPa-1 CONs directly.

AFM was used to measure the surface roughness of M1-M6. The 2D/3D scan photos were shown in Fig. 5 . The surface roughness parameters of average roughness (Ra) and root mean-square roughness (Rq) were shown in Table 1 . It could be found that the more the TpPa-1 CONs added, the rougher the TFN surface became. In addition, the increased roughness of the membrane surface also meant the successful implantation of TpPa-1 CONs. The rougher surface could provide more contact area for water transport and thus increase the permeability. However, the higher additions lead to defects during the IP process, resulting in a reduction in their rejection capacities.

It can be found in Table 1 that the surface hydrophilicity of the TFC membrane was not satisfactory, with a water contact angle (WCA) of around 88.6°. The surface of TpPa-1 CONs carries a small number of hydrophilic groups such as –OH, –NH2, therefore, the TFN membrane exhibits good hydrophilicity after adding TpPa-1 CONs into the TFC, with the WCA decreasing from 88.6° to around 48.6°. In conclusion, the incorporation of TpPa-1 CONs impacted the membrane with stronger negatively charged and hydrophilic, which helps to improve the separation performance of the membrane.

Since the separation performance of NF membranes is based on the size-sieving effect and the Donnan effect, the surface charge characteristics of the membrane are significant to the desalination performance of the membrane [47]. The zeta potential values of M1-M6 at different pH values (3–10) were shown in Fig. S9B. The electronegativity of the membrane surface gradually increased with the increase of pH value. Focusing on the electronegativity change when the potential is neutral (pH = 7, Table 1), it can be found that the electronegativity of the membrane surface also increases gradually with the addition of TpPa-1 CONs increasing from −3.75 mV of M1 to −24.83 mV of M6. Theoretically, due to the enhancement of the electronegativity of the membrane surface, the desalinization performance will also be enhanced. However, when the TpPa-1 CONs was added to a certain amount, the enhanced electronegativity would not enough to compensate the surface defects formed by the agglomeration of TpPa-1 CONs during the IP process, thus the Na2SO4 rejection still shows a downward trend.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of TpPa-1 CONs nanocomposite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: From the data in Fig. 7 A, both the pure water permeance and the Na2SO4 rejection increased with increasing the addition of TpPa-1 CONs. The abundance of oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups (such as –OH and –NH2) of TpPa-1 CONs can increase the hydrophilicity and accelerate the transport of water molecules. Meanwhile, the charge carried by these functional groups enhanced the surface electronegativity of the membrane, which in turn enhanced the Na2SO4 rejection. The intrinsic pore of TpPa-1 CONs provided extra channels for water molecules transport, which reduced the mass transfer resistance during water transfer. Furthermore, the increased surface roughness provided more water contact area to facilitate the diffusion of water molecules across the membrane surface. Thus, the pure water permeance was increased. However, when the addition concentration of TpPa-1 CONs was higher than 0.007 wt/v%, the water permeance started to decrease, which was mainly due to the accumulation of TpPa-1 CONs blocking the nanochannels. This phenomena also occurs when inorganic materials such as GO [15] and MoS2 [38] were used as active fillers. Among these TpPa-1 CONs nanocomposite membranes, M4 had the highest pure water flux, which was about 1.4 times larger than that of TFC membrane.

As to the Na2SO4 rejection, the incorporation of TpPa-1 CONs enhances the cross-linking degree and the electronegativity of the membrane surface, then the size exclusion and the Donnan effect [38] simultaneously have a positive impact on the separation of multivalent ions. In terms of salts rejection (Fig. 7B), the rejection of sulfate salts is higher than that of chloride salts. As for the two sulfates, the higher Na2SO4 rejection may be due to the electrostatic attraction of the negatively charged membrane which was weaker to Na + than to Mg2+ [3]. Generally as established, the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of NF membranes is 150–1000 Da [50]. After the PEG test, the MWCO of M1 and M4 were calculated to be 276 Da and 252 Da (Fig. 7C), the corresponding effective pore diameter (μp) of M1 and M4 were about 0.38 and 0.36 nm, respectively. Theoretically, there was little difference in the rejection rates of Cl− (3.32 Å) and SO4 2− (3.79 Å) at these pore size [3]. Since SO4 2− was a multivalent ion with a strong dehydration energy barrier, it is more difficult to lose the water carried on the surface than Cl−, so the rejection of SO4 2− is higher than that of Cl−. The permeance and rejection of M4 were higher than M1, which also reflected that the TpPa-1 CONs nanocomposite membrane had a high monovalent/divalent salt selectivity. Overall, the sequence of salt rejection was as follow: Na2SO4＞MgSO4＞MgCl2＞NaCl, which is a typical salt retention sequence of negatively charged NF membranes.

From the simulation results in Fig. 7D, the linear upward trend of M4 is better than M1, which indicates that the modified TFN membrane has a wider pressure applicability range. In Fig. 8 , the separation performance of M4 in this study was compared with other TFN membranes. Detailed information was given in Table 3 . The abundant intrastent transfer channels and the precise sieving based on the skeleton-pore make TpPa-1 CONs membrane has obvious advantage in the comprehensive performance comparison with high permeance and high Na2SO4 rejection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and anti-fouling performance of TpPa-1 CONs NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The NF membrane should not only have excellent salt separation performance, but also have long-term operation stability and superior anti-fouling performance. The stability and anti-fouling performance experiments of M1 and M4 were conducted at a pressure of 4.0 bar, respectively.

For the 35-days long-term operation stability experiment, the feed solution was 2000 ppm Na2SO4, the permeance of M1 and M4 were monitored by computer and the permeate flux was collected every 24 h to test the Na2SO4 rejection. As shown in Fig. 9 A, the permeate flux of M1 decreased about 17.5%, while M4 only decreased about 8.3% during the whole operation, with the latter mainly benefitting from the permanent pore of the TpPa-1 CONs. Simultaneously, Na2SO4 rejection of M4 declined on the 13th day, the overall decrease was slow and remained above 98% at the end of the test. On the contrary, M1 showed an obvious decrease in Na2SO4 rejection on day 6 and dropped to about 95% at the end of the test. The long-term operation stability of M4 indicated that there was good compatibility between TpPa-1 CONs and PA layer. The addition of TpPa-1 CONs could curb the deterioration of the membrane during nanofiltration effectively. The TFN-NF membrane modified by TpPa-1 CONs has high desalination performance and good stability, and has great potential for long-term operation.

The anti-fouling performance tests were performed on M1 and M4. The experimental conditions were maintained, and the feed solution in the buffer bottle was alternately changed to 1000 ppm BSA solution (pH = 6) an DI water (pH = 7). The rate of change of FRR demonstrates the anti-fouling performance of the membrane. Compared to M1, the FRR of M4 decreases slowly in BSA solution and can recover to 82.8% after DI rinsing, indicating that M4 exhibits better fouling resistance than M1.

In conclusion, we demonstrated a modification experiment of nanofiltration membrane by integrating TpPa-1 CONs into the PA layer after stripping. TpPa-1 CONs obtained by mechanical grinding method with different concentrations were dispersed in the organic phase of interfacial polymerization experiment. By dispersing TpPa-1 CONs in the organic phase to obtain a more uniformly dispersed solution, membrane defects due to the agglomeration of nanoparticles during interfacial polymerization are minimized. In addition, TpPa-1 CONs can not only improve the pure water permeance up to 8.54 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 (∼1.4 times than that of TFC membrane) with excellent Na2SO4 rejection of about 98.1% (Na2SO4 rejection of TFC was 96.5%), but also have a very important effect on the surface hydrophilicity, electronegativity, long-term operation stability and anti-fouling performance of the membrane. As a 2D porous nanosheets, TpPa-1 CONs provided some guidance for the preparation of long-term stability NF membranes with excellent separation performance.
